nodes.output:
OpenAI API v2.3.0 is a REST API (https://api.openai.com) with 241 endpoints covering assistants, core model usage, media, evals, governance, and organization/project management. Detailed per-endpoint docs are at `https://oapis.org/summary/openai/[idOrRoute]` and the main reference at `https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference`.

Key areas:

Assistants  
Build stateful assistants that call models and tools:  
- Manage assistants: list, create (with model + instructions), retrieve, modify, delete.  
- Manage threads (conversations): create, get, modify, delete.  
- Manage messages within threads: list, create, get, modify, delete.  
- Manage runs (executions of assistants on threads): list, create, get, modify, cancel in-progress runs.  
- Manage run steps: list, get.  
- Submit tool outputs for runs that require tool results (`status: requires_action`).

Audio  
Convert between text and audio, and manage voice consents/voices:  
- Text → speech: generate audio (file or streamed events).  
- Speech → text: transcription in source language, with JSON/verbose/diarized formats and streaming.  
- Speech → English: translation.  
- Voice consents: list, upload, get, update metadata, delete.  
- Custom voices: create a custom voice.

Audit Logs  
- List actions and configuration changes within an organization.

Batch  
Asynchronous large batches of API calls:  
- List batches, create/execute batch from uploaded file, retrieve batch status, cancel in-progress batches (which may yield partial output).

Certificates  
Manage TLS/client certificates at organization and project levels:  
- Organization: list, upload (does not auto-activate), activate/deactivate up to 10 at a time atomically, get (active or not), modify name, delete (must be inactive everywhere).  
- Project: list, activate/deactivate up to 10 at a time atomically.

Chat  
Legacy/stored Chat Completions; new projects should use Responses instead:  
- Stored chat completions: list (only when created with `store: true`), get, update metadata, delete.  
- Inspect stored chat completion messages.

Completions  
Legacy text completion API:  
- Create completion for a prompt, returning one or more completions; supports streaming.

Conversations  
Lightweight conversation management distinct from Assistants:  
- Conversation items: list, create, get, delete by conversation and item IDs.  
- Conversations: create, get, update, delete (items are not deleted with the conversation).

Embeddings  
Vector representations for ML/search:  
- Create embedding vectors from text.

Evals  
Define and run evaluations of models and configurations:  
- Evaluations: list, create (define criteria and datasource schema/config), get, update certain properties, delete.  
- Runs: list runs for an eval, create run (specify datasource and model configuration; datasource validated against schema), get run, cancel run, delete run.  
- Run outputs: list output items, get a specific output item.  
Docs: Evals guide in platform documentation.

Files  
Upload and manage files used by Assistants, Fine-tuning, and Batch:  
- List files, upload, retrieve metadata, delete (also removes from vector stores), and download file contents.  
- Limits: per-file up to 512 MB; up to 2.5 TB per project; no org-wide limit.  
- Assistants: supports up to ~2M tokens and specific file types.  
- Fine-tuning: `.jsonl` only with required formats for chat/completions fine-tuning.  
- Batch: `.jsonl` up to 200 MB with a specific request format.  
- Storage limits can be increased via OpenAI support.

Fine-tuning  
Manage fine-tuning and related permissions/graders:  
- Graders: run and validate graders.  
- Checkpoint permissions (admin API key only): list all permissions on a fine-tuned checkpoint; create permissions (share checkpoints across projects); delete permissions.  
- Fine-tuning jobs: list with pagination; create jobs (returns status and eventual fine-tuned model name); retrieve job info; cancel immediately; list checkpoints; list events (status updates); pause and resume jobs.  
Docs: model optimization guides.

Groups, Roles, and Access Control  
Organization-level groups and roles:  
- Group organization role assignments: list roles for a group; assign/unassign org roles to/from groups.  
- Group users: list users in a group; add/remove users from a group.  
- Groups: list, create, update, delete groups.  
- Roles (org + project): list roles; create, update, delete custom roles at org or project scope.

Invites  
Organization membership via invites:  
- List invites; create invite for a user (must accept to gain access); retrieve invite; delete invite (not possible after acceptance).

Models  
Model catalog management:  
- List available models with owner/availability details.  
- Retrieve specific model info including owner and permissions.  
- Delete fine-tuned models (Owner role required).

Moderations  
Safety classification for text and images:  
- Create moderation request to classify potentially harmful content, with guidance in moderation docs.

Projects and Project-Level Access  
Projects represent scoped containers for keys, users, groups, rate limits, etc.:  
- Projects: list, create (cannot be deleted; only archived), retrieve, modify, archive.  
- Project API keys: list, retrieve, delete (returns confirmation or error if it was a service account key).  
- Project rate limits: list per model, update.  
- Project service accounts: list, create (returns unredacted API key), retrieve, delete (fails if project is archived).  
- Project users: list, add (must already be in organization), retrieve, modify role, delete (fails if project archived).  
- Project groups: list groups with access, add group to project, remove group.  
- Project group role assignments: list roles for a group within a project; assign/unassign project roles to/from groups.  
- Project user role assignments: list roles for a user within a project; assign/unassign project roles to/from users.

Realtime  
WebRTC/SIP-based realtime interactions and sessions:  
- Calls: create Realtime API call over WebRTC (returns SDP answer); accept incoming SIP call; hang up active call; refer (transfer) active SIP call; reject incoming SIP call with SIP status.  
- Client auth: create short-lived Realtime client secrets with attached session config; create Realtime sessions that return ephemeral tokens (`client_secret`) for browser/mobile clients; create dedicated realtime transcription sessions similarly.

Responses  
Recommended unified endpoint for most new use cases:  
- Create a model response with text/image inputs to produce text or JSON outputs. Supports tool calling (custom code, web search, file search) and background responses.  
- Retrieve or delete a response by ID.  
- Cancel background responses (`background: true`).  
- List input items associated with a given response.

Skills  
Skill management for the current project:  
- List skills; create skill; get skill; update default version pointer; delete skill; download skill content (zip bundle).  
- Skill versions: list versions for a skill; create immutable skill version; get specific version.

GitHub’s v3 REST API (v1.1.4, `https://api.github.com`) exposes 1099 endpoints. The excerpt focuses on the `actions` group, which manages GitHub Actions configuration and resources at enterprise, organization, and repository levels. For each endpoint’s full schema/details, you can visit `https://oapis.org/summary/github/[idOrRoute]`.

Below is a condensed overview preserving the functional scope of each cluster of endpoints.

GitHub Actions high-level capabilities:

- Manage Actions cache (retention and storage limits, usage, listing, deletion) for enterprises, organizations, and repositories.
- Fully manage GitHub-hosted and self-hosted runners and runner groups (create, list, describe, update, delete, configure limits/specs/platforms/images, manage labels, registration/remove tokens, and membership in groups).
- Configure organization- and repository-level Actions permissions and workflow policies (allowed Actions/reusable workflows, default workflow permissions, artifact/log retention, fork PR and private fork workflow rules, external workflow access).
- Manage organization and repository secrets and variables, including which repositories can access org-level secrets/variables.
- Manage workflow runs and jobs (list, get details, logs, artifacts, approvals, pending deployments, cancellations, re-runs, usage).
- Manage OIDC customization for workflow tokens.

Details by area:

1) GitHub Actions cache limits and usage

4) Self-hosted runner groups and access (organization)

5) Self-hosted runners at organization level

- Runner applications and JIT configs:

6) Organization secrets and variables

7) Repository-level artifacts

llm
nodes.output:
OpenAPI 3.1 spec for the OpenAI REST API (version 2.3.0), hosted at `https://api.openai.com/v1`, secured via `ApiKeyAuth`. Terms, support, and MIT license are referenced from openai.com and GitHub.

The excerpt defines the `POST /chat/completions` endpoint (`operationId: createChatCompletion`, tag `Chat`):

- Purpose: Creates a model response for a chat conversation (text, vision, audio). Supports streaming (returns either a single chat completion object or a stream of chunk objects).
- Recommendation: For new projects, use the `Responses` API instead; includes links comparing Chat Completions vs Responses and to guides for text generation, vision, audio, and reasoning.
- Note: Parameter support may differ by model, especially for newer reasoning models; unsupported options are documented in the reasoning guide.

Request body (JSON, required) is an object composed of base options plus chat-specific fields, with some parameters shared with reasoning models:

General request properties:
- `metadata`: optional object or null; up to 16 key-value pairs (key max length 64 chars, value max length 512 chars). Used to store structured extra info and to query via API or dashboard.
- `top_logprobs`: integer 0–20 or null; number of most likely tokens to return at each token position with log probabilities. (Defined twice: once in a general object with anyOf, and again in a second object directly as integer 0–20.)
- `temperature`: number or null, 0–2 (default 1). Controls sampling randomness: higher (e.g., 0.8) → more random, lower (e.g., 0.2) → more deterministic. Recommended to tune either `temperature` or `top_p`, not both.
- `top_p`: number or null, 0–1 (default 1). Nucleus sampling: considers smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability mass is `top_p` (e.g., 0.1 = top 10% mass). Also recommended not to tune simultaneously with `temperature`.
- `user`: string, deprecated. Formerly a stable identifier for end-users, used for caching and abuse detection. Now replaced by `safety_identifier` and `prompt_cache_key`. Keeping it can help cache hit rates; documentation links to safety best practices.
- `safety_identifier`: string (max length 64). Stable identifier to help detect policy-violating users. Should uniquely identify each user; recommended to hash username/email to avoid sending PII; referenced in safety best practices.
- `prompt_cache_key`: string. Used by OpenAI to cache responses for similar requests and improve cache hit rates. Replaces the `user` field; documented in prompt caching guide.
- `service_tier`: string or null; enum `auto | default | flex | scale | priority` (default `auto`). Determines processing mode and pricing:
  - `auto`: use project-level configured tier (usually `default`).
  - `default`: standard pricing/performance.
  - `flex` or `priority`: use corresponding service tier options (flex/priority processing).
  - If set, response also includes the `service_tier` actually used, which may differ from the requested one.
- `prompt_cache_retention`: string or null; enum `in-memory | 24h`. Policy for prompt cache retention. `24h` enables extended caching of prompt prefixes for up to 24 hours.

Core chat-specific property:
- `messages`: required array of chat messages (min 1). Represents the conversation so far. Supported content types depend on the model (text, images, audio). Each element is one of several message types, distinguished by `role` and structure:

1) Developer message:
- `role`: `developer` (required).
- `content`: required; either:
  - string; or
  - array of content parts, each a “Text content part”:
    - object with:
      - `type`: `"text"` (required; constant).
      - `text`: string (required).
- `name`: optional string, used to distinguish participants with same role.
- Semantics: Developer-provided instructions that the model should always follow. For `o1` and newer models, `developer` messages replace `system` messages.

2) System message:
- `role`: `system` (required).
- `content`: required; either:
  - string; or
  - array of content parts; for system messages, only text parts are allowed (same `Text content part` as above).
- `name`: optional string for participant identification.
- Semantics: Developer-provided instructions the model should follow, regardless of user messages. For new (`o1`) models, `developer` messages should be used instead.

3) User message:
- `role`: `user` (required).
- `content`: required; either:
  - string (text contents); or
  - array of content parts (min 1). Supported parts (model-dependent):
    - Text content part (same as above).
    - Image content part:
      - `type`: `"image_url"` (constant).
      - `image_url`: object with:
        - `url`: string (uri). Either a URL or base64-encoded image data.
        - `detail`: optional string, enum `auto | low | high` (default `auto`). Controls image detail level; documented in vision guide.
    - Audio content part:
      - `type`: `"input_audio"` (constant).
      - `input_audio`: object with:
        - `data`: string, base64-encoded audio.
        - `format`: string, enum `wav | mp3`, specifying audio format.
    - File content part:
      - `type`: `"file"` (constant).
      - `file`: object with:
        - `filename`: string, for passing file as a string.
        - `file_data`: string, base64-encoded file data, used when passing as a string.
        - `file_id`: string, ID of an uploaded file to use as input.
- `name`: optional participant identifier.
- Semantics: End-user messages with prompts or additional context.

4) Assistant message:
- `role`: `assistant` (required).
- `content`: anyOf:
  - either:
    - string; or
    - array of content parts (min 1). For assistant messages:
      - Text content parts (same as above), and/or
      - Refusal content part:
        - `type`: `"refusal"` (constant).
        - `refusal`: string containing the refusal message.
      - Discriminator on `type`.
  - or `null`.
- `refusal`: string or null; explicit refusal message by assistant.
- `name`: optional string.
- `audio`: optional object with details about prior audio responses, including:
  - `id`: required string (ID of previous audio response from the model). Further properties continue beyond the excerpt.
- Semantics: Model replies to user messages. `content` is required unless tool or function calls are used (not fully included in the excerpt).

Additional notes:
- Many descriptions link to OpenAI documentation for detailed guides on text generation, vision, audio, safety identifiers, and prompt caching.
- Some newer reasoning-model-specific parameters and limitations are mentioned, with a pointer to the reasoning guide.
- The schema uses `anyOf`, `oneOf`, `required`, enums, and discriminators to model the different message roles and content part types.

nodes.name: llm
nodes.output:
The document is an OpenAPI 3.1 spec for the legacy `/v1/completions` endpoint of the OpenAI API.

Core metadata
- API name: OpenAI API; version: `2.3.0`.
- Base URL: `https://api.openai.com/v1`.
- Auth: `ApiKeyAuth` via `Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY`.
- Terms: `https://openai.com/policies/terms-of-use`; docs at `https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference`.

Endpoint: `POST /completions`
- Purpose: Create a text completion for a given prompt and parameters.
- Returns: a completion object; can be single response or streamed Server-Sent Events (SSE).

Request body (JSON)
- `model` (required):
  - ID of completion model. String; may be one of `gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct`, `davinci-002`, `babbage-002`, or any valid model ID.
- `prompt` (required):
  - Text to complete. Supports:
    - string, array of strings;
    - array of token IDs (integers);
    - array of arrays of token IDs.
  - Default: `<|endoftext|>` if not specified; `""` examples for string and array.
- `best_of` (int, default 1, 0–20, nullable):
  - Server-side generates `best_of` candidates and returns the single best by log probability.
  - Incompatible with streaming.
  - If used with `n`, `best_of` is candidates count; `n` is number of results returned; `best_of > n`.
  - Can consume many tokens; tune with `max_tokens` and `stop`.
- `echo` (bool, default `false`, nullable):
  - If true, response includes the prompt in addition to completion.
- `frequency_penalty` (number -2 to 2, default 0, nullable):
  - Positive values penalize token repetition based on frequency, reducing verbatim repeats.
- `logit_bias` (object map<string,int>, default `null`, nullable):
  - Per-token-ID bias (-100 to 100) applied to logits before sampling.
  - Can ban or strongly prefer tokens (e.g. `{"50256": -100}` to prevent `<|endoftext|>`).
- `logprobs` (int 0–5, default `null`, nullable):
  - Include log probabilities for the `logprobs` most likely tokens plus the chosen token.
  - Up to `logprobs + 1` entries per position.
- `max_tokens` (int ≥ 0, default 16, nullable):
  - Max tokens to generate in completion.
  - `prompt tokens + max_tokens` must be ≤ model context length.
- `n` (int 1–128, default 1, nullable):
  - Number of completions to generate per prompt.
  - Large values consume more tokens; use with care.
- `presence_penalty` (number -2 to 2, default 0, nullable):
  - Positive values penalize tokens that already appear, encouraging new topics.
- `seed` (int64, nullable):
  - Attempts deterministic sampling across identical requests.
  - Determinism not guaranteed; use with `system_fingerprint` to detect backend changes.
- `stop` (string or array of strings, up to 4, nullable, default `null`):
  - Not supported with reasoning models `o3` and `o4-mini`.
  - Sequences where generation stops; stop sequence is omitted from returned text.
  - Single string variant default is `<|endoftext|>`.
- `stream` (bool, default `false`, nullable):
  - If true, responses are streamed as SSE chunks with `data: [DONE]` terminator.
- `stream_options` (object or null):
  - Only use when `stream: true`.
  - `include_usage` (bool):
    - If true, an extra final chunk (before `[DONE]`) has `usage` for the entire request; other chunks have `usage: null`.
    - If stream interrupts early, final usage chunk may not arrive.
  - `include_obfuscation` (bool):
    - When true (default behavior), streaming deltas contain extra `obfuscation` characters to normalize payload size and mitigate side-channel attacks.
    - Set false to save bandwidth if network is trusted.
- `suffix` (string, nullable, default `null`):
  - Suffix that comes after inserted completion.
  - Only supported for `gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct`.
- `temperature` (number 0–2, default 1, nullable):
  - Sampling temperature: higher = more random, lower = more deterministic.
  - Typically tune `temperature` or `top_p`, but not both.
- `top_p` (number 0–1, default 1, nullable):
  - Nucleus sampling. `0.1` = restrict to top 10% probability mass.
  - Generally adjust `top_p` or `temperature`, not both.
- `user` (string):
  - End-user identifier for abuse monitoring; recommended for safety.

Response: completion object (200 OK)
- Shape is the same for streamed and non-streamed responses.
- Top-level fields:
  - `id` (string): Unique completion ID.
  - `object` (string): Always `"text_completion"`.
  - `created` (int): Unix timestamp (seconds).
  - `model` (string): Model used.
  - `system_fingerprint` (string):
    - Identifier for backend configuration used for this completion.
    - Combine with `seed` to understand determinism changes over time.
  - `choices` (array of objects):
    - Each choice has:
      - `text` (string): Generated text.
      - `index` (int): Position in choices array.
      - `finish_reason` (string):
        - `stop`: hit natural or specified stop sequence.
        - `length`: hit `max_tokens`.
        - `content_filter`: content removed by filters.
      - `logprobs` (object or null):
        - When present:
          - `text_offset` (int[]).
          - `token_logprobs` (number[]).
          - `tokens` (string[]).
          - `top_logprobs` (array of objects mapping token → logprob).
  - `usage` (object):
    - Overall token usage:
      - `prompt_tokens` (int): Tokens in prompt.
      - `completion_tokens` (int): Tokens in completion.
      - `total_tokens` (int): Sum of prompt and completion tokens.
    - `completion_tokens_details` (object):
      - `accepted_prediction_tokens` (int): Predicted-output tokens that were used.
      - `audio_tokens` (int): Audio tokens generated.
      - `reasoning_tokens` (int): Tokens used internally for reasoning.
      - `rejected_prediction_tokens` (int):
        - Predicted tokens that did not appear in the completion but still counted for billing/output/context.
    - `prompt_tokens_details` (object):
      - `audio_tokens` (int): Audio tokens present in prompt.
      - `cached_tokens` (int): Cached tokens reused from prior context.

Example completion object (non-streamed)
- Typical response:
  - `id`: `"cmpl-uqkvlQyYK7bGYrRHQ0eXlWi7"`.
  - `object`: `"text_completion"`.
  - `created`: `1589478378`.
  - `model`: e.g. `"gpt-4-turbo"` or generic `VAR_completion_model_id`.
  - `system_fingerprint`: e.g. `"fp_44709d6fcb"`.
  - `choices`: single item with `text`, `index: 0`, `logprobs: null`, `finish_reason: "length"`.
  - `usage`: `prompt_tokens: 5`, `completion_tokens: 7`, `total_tokens: 12`.

x-oaiMeta / examples
- Endpoint metadata:
  - Name: `Create completion`; group: `completions`; marked `legacy: true`.
- Example: Non-streaming request
  - `curl`:
    - POST to `/v1/completions` with headers `Content-Type: application/json` and `Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY`.
    - Body: `{"model": "VAR_completion_model_id", "prompt": "Say this is a test", "max_tokens": 7, "temperature": 0}`.
  - Python:
    - Use `openai` (new client API `OpenAI`).
    - Call `client.completions.create(model="string", prompt="This is a test.")` and iterate over results.
  - JavaScript / Node:
    - `import OpenAI from "openai";`
    - `const openai = new OpenAI();`
    - For non-streaming: `openai.completions.create({ model, prompt, max_tokens: 7, temperature: 0 });`.
  - Go:
    - `github.com/openai/openai-go` with `Completions.New` and typed params.
  - Java:
    - `OpenAIOkHttpClient.fromEnv()`; build `CompletionCreateParams` with model `GPT_3_5_TURBO_INSTRUCT` and prompt.
  - Ruby:
    - `OpenAI::Client.new(api_key: "My API Key")`; call `openai.completions.create(model: :"gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct", prompt: "This is a test.")`.

- Example: Streaming request
  - `curl`:
    - Same endpoint but body includes `"stream": true`.
  - Python:
    - Iterate over `client.completions.create(...)` stream yields.
  - JavaScript:
    - `const stream = await openai.completions.create({ model, prompt, stream: true });`
    - `for await (const chunk of stream) { console.log(chunk.choices[0].text); }`.

Overall
- This spec defines the JSON schema, parameters, and response structure for the legacy `/completions` text-completion endpoint, including advanced sampling controls, streaming options, and token usage reporting.

nodes.output:
OpenAPI 3.1 spec for the OpenAI REST API (version 2.3.0).

- `info`:  
  - Title `OpenAI API`, REST interface to OpenAI; docs at `https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference`.  
  - Terms: `https://openai.com/policies/terms-of-use`.  
  - Contact: OpenAI Support (`https://help.openai.com/`).  
  - License: MIT, repo `https://github.com/openai/openai-openapi/blob/master/LICENSE`.

- `servers`:  
  - Base URL: `https://api.openai.com/v1`.

- `security`:  
  - Uses `ApiKeyAuth` (API key auth).

- Endpoint `/embeddings` (POST, `operationId: createEmbedding`, tag `Embeddings`):  
  - Summary: Creates an embedding vector representing the input text.  

  Request body (`application/json`, required, no additional properties):  
  - `model` (required):  
    - ID of the embedding model to use.  
    - Any string, with documented enum values `text-embedding-ada-002`, `text-embedding-3-small`, `text-embedding-3-large`.  
    - See Models list or Model overview docs.  
  - `input` (required): text or tokens to embed.  
    - Must not be empty; each item must be ≤ 8192 input tokens; arrays must have ≤ 2048 items.  
    - All inputs combined must be ≤ 300,000 tokens per request.  
    - Supported shapes (`oneOf`):  
      - Single string (will be embedded).  
      - Array of strings (multiple inputs).  
      - Array of integers (token ids for one input).  
      - Array of arrays of integers (multiple inputs as token-id arrays).  
    - Examples: `"This is a test."`, `["This is a test."]`, `[1212, 318, 257, 1332, 13]`, `[[1212, 318, 257, 1332, 13]]`.  
    - Token counting example code linked from the cookbook.  
  - `encoding_format` (optional):  
    - Format of returned embeddings: `float` or `base64` (default `float`).  
  - `dimensions` (optional):  
    - Integer ≥ 1; desired dimensionality of the output embedding.  
    - Only supported for `text-embedding-3` models and later.  
  - `user` (optional string):  
    - End-user identifier for abuse monitoring; see safety best practices.

  Response `200 OK` (`application/json`):  
  - Top-level object with required fields: `object`, `model`, `data`, `usage`.  
  - `object`: always `"list"`.  
  - `model`: string name of the model used.  
  - `data`: array of embedding objects:  
    - Each embedding object:  
      - `index` (integer): index of embedding in the list.  
      - `object`: `"embedding"`.  
      - `embedding`: array of floats (vector). Length depends on model; e.g., 1536 floats for `text-embedding-ada-002`.  
  - `usage`:  
    - `prompt_tokens` (integer): tokens used in the prompt.  
    - `total_tokens` (integer): total tokens used.

  Example request (curl):  
  - POST to `https://api.openai.com/v1/embeddings` with `Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY` and JSON body:
    - `{"input": "The food was delicious and the waiter...", "model": "text-embedding-ada-002", "encoding_format": "float"}`.

  Example SDK usage:  
  - Python: `client.embeddings.create(input="The quick brown fox jumped over the lazy dog", model="text-embedding-3-small")`.  
  - JavaScript/Node: `openai.embeddings.create({ model: "text-embedding-ada-002", input: "The quick brown fox..." , encoding_format: "float"})`.  
  - C#: `EmbeddingClient` with model `text-embedding-3-small`, then `GenerateEmbedding`.  
  - Node.js (alt): `client.embeddings.create({ input: 'The quick brown fox...', model: 'text-embedding-3-small' })`.  
  - Go: `client.Embeddings.New` with `EmbeddingNewParams` and `EmbeddingModelTextEmbedding3Small`.  
  - Java: `client.embeddings().create` with `EmbeddingModel.TEXT_EMBEDDING_3_SMALL`.  
  - Ruby: `openai.embeddings.create(input: "The quick brown fox...", model: :"text-embedding-3-small")`.

  Example response:  
  - `{"object": "list","data":[{"object":"embedding","embedding":[0.0023064255,-0.009327292,...,-0.0028842222],"index":0}],"model":"text-embedding-ada-002","usage":{"prompt_tokens":8,"total_tokens":8}}`.

- Top-level `x-oaiMeta`:  
  - `navigationGroups`: high-level documentation groupings:  
    - `responses` (Responses API)  
    - `webhooks` (Webhooks)  
    - `endpoints` (Platform APIs)  
    - `vector_stores` (Vector stores)  
    - `chatkit` (ChatKit, beta)  
    - `containers` (Containers)  
    - `realtime` (Realtime)  
    - `chat` (Chat Completions)  
    - `assistants` (Assistants, deprecated)  
    - `administration` (Administration)  
    - `legacy` (Legacy)

  - `groups`: detailed event/section groupings for docs:

    - `responses-streaming` (Streaming events, navigationGroup `responses`):  
      - Describes server-sent events emitted when creating a Response with `stream: true`.  
      - References event objects like `ResponseCreatedEvent`, `ResponseInProgressEvent`, `ResponseCompletedEvent`, `ResponseFailedEvent`, `ResponseIncompleteEvent`, item/content events, text/refusal delta/done events, function call argument events, file search and web search call events, reasoning summary/text events, image generation call events, MCP call and tool-listing events, code interpreter events, output text annotation events, `ResponseQueuedEvent`, custom tool call input events, and `ResponseErrorEvent`.  
      - Some paths are explicit (e.g., `response/output_text/delta`, `response/output_text/done`), others `<auto>`.

    - `webhook-events` (Webhook Events, navigationGroup `webhooks`):  
      - Explains that OpenAI sends HTTP requests (webhooks) to a specified URL on certain events; link to webhook guide.  
      - Events include: `WebhookResponseCompleted`, `WebhookResponseCancelled`, `WebhookResponseFailed`, `WebhookResponseIncomplete`, `WebhookBatchCompleted`, `WebhookBatchCancelled`, `WebhookBatchExpired`, `WebhookBatchFailed`, `WebhookFineTuningJobSucceeded`, `WebhookFineTuningJobFailed`, `WebhookFineTuningJobCancelled`, `WebhookEvalRunSucceeded`, `WebhookEvalRunFailed`, `WebhookEvalRunCanceled`, `WebhookRealtimeCallIncoming`.

    - `images-streaming` (Image Streaming, navigationGroup `endpoints`):  
      - Streaming image generation/editing via server-sent events; link to image-generation guide.  
      - Sections: `ImageGenPartialImageEvent`, `ImageGenCompletedEvent`, `ImageEditPartialImageEvent`, `ImageEditCompletedEvent`.

    - `realtime-client-events` (Client events, navigationGroup `realtime`):  
      - Lists events accepted by the Realtime WebSocket server from the client:  
        - `RealtimeClientEventSessionUpdate`, `RealtimeClientEventInputAudioBufferAppend`, `...Commit`, `...Clear`, conversation item create/retrieve/truncate/delete, response create/cancel, output audio buffer clear.

    - `realtime-server-events` (Server events, navigationGroup `realtime`):  
      - Events emitted by the Realtime WebSocket server to the client, including:  
        - Errors, session created/updated; conversation item added/done/retrieved/truncated/deleted and audio transcription states (completed/delta/segment/failed).  
        - Input audio buffer events (committed, DTMF received, cleared, speech started/stopped, timeout).  
        - Output audio buffer events (started/stopped/cleared).  
        - Response lifecycle events (created/done, output item added/done, content part added/done, text delta/done, audio transcript delta/done, audio delta/done).  
        - Function call arguments events (delta/done).  
        - MCP call argument events and lifecycle (in progress/completed/failed); MCP list tools in progress/completed/failed.  
        - `RealtimeServerEventRateLimitsUpdated`.

    - `chat-streaming` (Streaming, navigationGroup `chat`):  
      - Describes streaming Chat Completions via server-sent events; linked to streaming responses guide with `api-mode=chat`.  
      - Section: `CreateChatCompletionStreamResponse` at path `streaming`.

    - `assistants-streaming` (Streaming, beta, navigationGroup `assistants`):  
      - Streaming for Assistants API: stream events from Create Thread and Run, Create Run, and Submit Tool Outputs by passing `"stream": true`.  
      - Response is an SSE stream; SDKs (Node, Python) provide helpers; points to Assistants API quickstart.  
      - Section: `AssistantStreamEvent` at path `events`.

    - `realtime-beta-client-events` (Realtime Beta client events, navigationGroup `legacy`):  
      - Similar to realtime client events, but for beta/legacy: events the Realtime Beta WebSocket server accepts from clients, like `RealtimeBetaClientEventSessionUpdate`, `RealtimeBetaClientEventInputAudioBufferAppend`, etc.

nodes.output:
OpenAI API OpenAPI spec (v3.1.0), titled “OpenAI API” (version 2.3.0), describes the REST API at `https://api.openai.com/v1`. It uses API key security (`ApiKeyAuth`) and is licensed under MIT. Docs and terms are at `https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference` and `https://openai.com/policies/terms-of-use`.

The main defined endpoint is:

/audio/speech (POST, operationId: `createSpeech`, tag: Audio)
- Purpose: Generate speech audio from input text, returning either an audio file or a stream of audio events.
- Request body: JSON object (no additional properties) with:
  - `model` (required): TTS model to use. Accepts:
    - Any string, with recommended/known values:
      - `tts-1`
      - `tts-1-hd`
      - `gpt-4o-mini-tts`
      - `gpt-4o-mini-tts-2025-12-15`
  - `input` (required): Text to convert to speech.
    - Type: string
    - Max length: 4096 characters
  - `instructions` (optional):
    - Type: string, max length 4096
    - Additional guidance to control voice characteristics.
    - Not supported with `tts-1` or `tts-1-hd`.
  - `voice` (required): Voice selection.
    - Either:
      - Built-in voice name (string), examples and allowed enum:
        - `alloy`, `ash`, `ballad`, `coral`, `echo`, `sage`, `shimmer`, `verse`, `marin`, `cedar`
      - Or custom voice reference object:
        - Type: object, no additional properties
        - Required property:
          - `id` (string): custom voice ID, e.g. `voice_1234`
  - `response_format` (optional):
    - Audio file format for the response.
    - Type: string, default `mp3`
    - Allowed values: `mp3`, `opus`, `aac`, `flac`, `wav`, `pcm`
  - `speed` (optional):
    - Type: number, default `1`
    - Range: `0.25` to `4.0`
    - Controls playback speed (1.0 = normal).
  - `stream_format` (optional):
    - How audio is streamed if streaming is used.
    - Type: string, default `audio`
    - Allowed: `sse`, `audio`
    - `sse` not supported for models `tts-1` or `tts-1-hd`.

Responses for /audio/speech
- HTTP 200:
  - Headers:
    - `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` (string header)
  - Content types:
    - `application/octet-stream`:
      - Binary audio data (`string`, `format: binary`) for non-streaming scenario.
    - `text/event-stream`:
      - Server-sent events representing streaming audio and completion state.
      - Two event object shapes, differentiated by `type` (discriminator: `propertyName: type`):

1) `speech.audio.delta` event
  - Description: emitted for each chunk of audio data during speech synthesis.
  - Properties:
    - `type` (string, required):
      - Always `speech.audio.delta` (enum single value).
    - `audio` (string, required):
      - Base64-encoded audio data chunk.
  - Metadata example:
    - `{ "type": "speech.audio.delta", "audio": "base64-encoded-audio-data" }`

2) `speech.audio.done` event
  - Description: emitted when speech synthesis completes and all audio is streamed.
  - Properties:
    - `type` (string, required):
      - Always `speech.audio.done`.
    - `usage` (object, required):
      - Token usage statistics:
        - `input_tokens` (integer): number of input tokens.
        - `output_tokens` (integer): number of output tokens generated.
        - `total_tokens` (integer): sum of input and output tokens.
  - Example:
    - `{"type":"speech.audio.done","usage":{"input_tokens":14,"output_tokens":101,"total_tokens":115}}`

Examples for /audio/speech (x-oaiMeta)
- Named example: “Create speech”, group: `audio`.

1) Example: “Default”
- Generic description: show how to call `/audio/speech` and save or inspect output.

- curl:
  - POST `https://api.openai.com/v1/audio/speech`
  - Headers: `Authorization: Bearer $OPENAI_API_KEY`, `Content-Type: application/json`
  - Body:
    - `{"model": "gpt-4o-mini-tts", "input": "The quick brown fox jumped over the lazy dog.", "voice": "alloy"}`
  - Writes output directly to `speech.mp3` using `--output` option.

- Python:
  - Uses `openai` library (`from openai import OpenAI`).
  - Creates `OpenAI` client, optionally passing `api_key` from `OPENAI_API_KEY` (default can be omitted).
  - Calls `client.audio.speech.create(input="input", model="string", voice="string")`.
  - Prints `speech` object, reads content via `speech.read()`, prints content.

- JavaScript (browser/Node with ES modules):
  - Imports `fs`, `path`, and `OpenAI`.
  - Creates `OpenAI` instance with default configuration.
  - Sets output path `./speech.mp3`.
  - Async `main` function:
    - Calls `openai.audio.speech.create({ model: "gpt-4o-mini-tts", voice: "alloy", input: "Today is a wonderful day to build something people love!" })`.
    - Converts response to ArrayBuffer, then to `Buffer`, writes to `speech.mp3`.

- C#:
  - Uses `OpenAI.Audio` and `BinaryData`.
  - Creates `AudioClient` with `model: "gpt-4o-mini-tts"` and `apiKey` from `OPENAI_API_KEY`.
  - Calls `client.GenerateSpeech(text: "...", voice: GeneratedSpeechVoice.Alloy)`.
  - Writes `BinaryData` to `speech.mp3` via `FileStream`.

- Node.js (OpenAI Node client):
  - Imports `OpenAI`.
  - Configures client with `apiKey: process.env['OPENAI_API_KEY']` (default).
  - Calls `client.audio.speech.create({ input: 'input', model: 'string', voice: 'string' })`.
  - Logs `speech`, then `await speech.blob()` to get content.

- Go:
  - Uses `github.com/openai/openai-go` and `github.com/openai/openai-go/option`.
  - Creates client with `option.WithAPIKey("My API Key")`.
  - Calls `client.Audio.Speech.New` with params: `Input: "input"`, `Model: openai.SpeechModelTTS1`, `Voice` union holding string voice.
  - On success, prints `speech`; on error, panics.

- Java:
  - Uses `OpenAIClient` (`OpenAIOkHttpClient.fromEnv()`).
  - Builds `SpeechCreateParams` with `.input("input")`, `.model(SpeechModel.TTS_1)`, `.voice("string")`.
  - Calls `client.audio().speech().create(params)` to get `HttpResponse`.

- Ruby:
  - `require "openai"`.
  - `openai = OpenAI::Client.new(api_key: "My API Key")`.
  - Calls `openai.audio.speech.create(input: "input", model: :"tts-1", voice: "string")`.
  - Prints `speech`.

2) Example: “SSE Stream Format”
- Demonstrates enabling server-sent events via `stream_format: "sse"` in the request body.

- curl:
  - Similar to default example but includes `"stream_format": "sse"` and same model/voice sample.

- Node.js, Python, Go, Java, Ruby examples:
  - Similar to the default examples (same placeholder input/model/voice usage).
  - They illustrate using the same `client.audio.speech.create` (or language-specific analog) patterns.
  - The OpenAPI example focuses on the `stream_format` property but example code snippets themselves do not explicitly set it at SDK level; they serve as basic API usage references.

x-oaiMeta (global metadata)
- `navigationGroups`: high-level doc sections for the OpenAI platform:
  - `responses` – “Responses API”
  - `webhooks` – “Webhooks”
  - `endpoints` – “Platform APIs”
  - `vector_stores` – “Vector stores”
  - `chatkit` (beta) – “ChatKit”
  - `containers` – “Containers”
  - `realtime` – “Realtime”
  - `chat` – “Chat Completions”
  - `assistants` – “Assistants” (deprecated)
  - `administration` – “Administration”
  - `legacy` – “Legacy”

- `groups`: detailed documentation groups with sections (events and objects). Only the structure is defined here; actual schemas are elsewhere.

1) Group: `responses-streaming`
- Title: “Streaming events”
- Navigation group: `responses`
- Description: When creating a Response with `stream: true`, the server emits server-sent events as the Response is generated. This group enumerates those events.
- Sections (each `type: object` with `key` and `path`):
  - `ResponseCreatedEvent`
  - `ResponseInProgressEvent`
  - `ResponseCompletedEvent`
  - `ResponseFailedEvent`
  - `ResponseIncompleteEvent`
  - `ResponseOutputItemAddedEvent`
  - `ResponseOutputItemDoneEvent`
  - `ResponseContentPartAddedEvent`
  - `ResponseContentPartDoneEvent`
  - `ResponseTextDeltaEvent` (path: `response/output_text/delta`)
  - `ResponseTextDoneEvent` (path: `response/output_text/done`)
  - `ResponseRefusalDeltaEvent`
  - `ResponseRefusalDoneEvent`
  - `ResponseFunctionCallArgumentsDeltaEvent`
  - `ResponseFunctionCallArgumentsDoneEvent`
  - `ResponseFileSearchCallInProgressEvent`
  - `ResponseFileSearchCallSearchingEvent`
  - `ResponseFileSearchCallCompletedEvent`
  - `ResponseWebSearchCallInProgressEvent`
  - `ResponseWebSearchCallSearchingEvent`
  - `ResponseWebSearchCallCompletedEvent`
  - `ResponseReasoningSummaryPartAddedEvent`
  - `ResponseReasoningSummaryPartDoneEvent`
  - `ResponseReasoningSummaryTextDeltaEvent`
  - `ResponseReasoningSummaryTextDoneEvent`
  - `ResponseReasoningTextDeltaEvent`
  - `ResponseReasoningTextDoneEvent`
  - `ResponseImageGenCallCompletedEvent`
  - `ResponseImageGenCallGeneratingEvent`
  - `ResponseImageGenCallInProgressEvent`
  - `ResponseImageGenCallPartialImageEvent`
  - `ResponseMCPCallArgumentsDeltaEvent`
  - `ResponseMCPCallArgumentsDoneEvent`
  - `ResponseMCPCallCompletedEvent`
  - `ResponseMCPCallFailedEvent`
  - `ResponseMCPCallInProgressEvent`
  - `ResponseMCPListToolsCompletedEvent`
  - `ResponseMCPListToolsFailedEvent`
  - `ResponseMCPListToolsInProgressEvent`
  - `ResponseCodeInterpreterCallInProgressEvent`
  - `ResponseCodeInterpreterCallInterpretingEvent`
  - `ResponseCodeInterpreterCallCompletedEvent`
  - `ResponseCodeInterpreterCallCodeDeltaEvent`
  - `ResponseCodeInterpreterCallCodeDoneEvent`
  - `ResponseOutputTextAnnotationAddedEvent`
  - `ResponseQueuedEvent`
  - `ResponseCustomToolCallInputDeltaEvent`
  - `ResponseCustomToolCallInputDoneEvent`
  - `ResponseErrorEvent`

2) Group: `webhook-events`
- Title: “Webhook Events”
- Navigation group: `webhooks`
- Description: Webhooks are HTTP requests sent by OpenAI to user-defined URLs when specific events occur during API usage. Links to webhook guide.
- Sections:
  - `WebhookResponseCompleted`
  - `WebhookResponseCancelled`
  - `WebhookResponseFailed`
  - `WebhookResponseIncomplete`
  - `WebhookBatchCompleted`
  - `WebhookBatchCancelled`
  - `WebhookBatchExpired`
  - `WebhookBatchFailed`
  - `WebhookFineTuningJobSucceeded`
  - `WebhookFineTuningJobFailed`
  - `WebhookFineTuningJobCancelled`
  - `WebhookEvalRunSucceeded`
  - `WebhookEvalRunFailed`
  - `WebhookEvalRunCanceled`
  - `WebhookRealtimeCallIncoming`

3) Group: `images-streaming`
- Title: “Image Streaming”
- Navigation group: `endpoints`
- Description: Streaming image generation and editing in real time via server-sent events; links to image generation guide.
- Sections:
  - `ImageGenPartialImageEvent`
  - `ImageGenCompletedEvent`
  - `ImageEditPartialImageEvent`
  - `ImageEditCompletedEvent`

4) Group: `realtime-client-events`
- Title: “Client events”
- Navigation group: `realtime`
- Description: Events that the Realtime WebSocket server accepts from clients.
- Sections:
  - `RealtimeClientEventSessionUpdate`
  - `RealtimeClientEventInputAudioBufferAppend`
  - `RealtimeClientEventInputAudioBufferCommit`
  - `RealtimeClientEventInputAudioBufferClear`
  - `RealtimeClientEventConversationItemCreate`
  - `RealtimeClientEventConversationItemRetrieve`
  - `RealtimeClientEventConversationItemTruncate`
  - `RealtimeClientEventConversationItemDelete`
  - `RealtimeClientEventResponseCreate`
  - `RealtimeClientEventResponseCancel`
  - `RealtimeClientEventOutputAudioBufferClear`

5) Group: `realtime-server-events`

nodes.output:
GitHub v3 REST API – OpenAPI Overview (Excerpt)

- `openapi: 3.0.3`
- API info:
  - `version: 1.1.4`
  - `title: GitHub v3 REST API`
  - `description: GitHub's v3 REST API.`
  - License: MIT (`https://spdx.org/licenses/MIT`)
  - Terms of service: `https://docs.github.com/articles/github-terms-of-service`
  - Contact: Support (`https://support.github.com/contact?tags=dotcom-rest-api`)
  - `x-github-plan: api.github.com`
- Servers:
  - `https://api.github.com`
- External docs: `https://docs.github.com/rest/`
- `paths: {}` (no standard REST paths defined in this snippet)

Webhook definition (`x-webhooks`):

Event: `branch-protection-configuration-disabled`
- HTTP method: `POST`
- Summary: Triggered when there is a change to branch protection configurations for a repository, specifically when all branch protections are disabled.
- Description: "All branch protections were disabled for a repository."
- Related docs:
  - Webhook event docs:  
    `https://docs.github.com/webhooks/webhook-events-and-payloads#branch_protection_configuration`
  - About protected branches:  
    `https://docs.github.com/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/defining-the-mergeability-of-pull-requests/about-protected-branches`
  - Managing branch protection via API:  
    - GraphQL `BranchProtectionRule`:  
      `https://docs.github.com/graphql/reference/objects#branchprotectionrule`
    - REST `Branch protection`:  
      `https://docs.github.com/rest/branches/branch-protection`
- Permissions requirement: To subscribe, a GitHub App must have at least read-level access for the `Administration` repository permission.
- `operationId`: `branch-protection-configuration/disabled`

Request:

Headers (`parameters`):
- `User-Agent` (header, string, example `GitHub-Hookshot/123abc`)
- `X-Github-Hook-Id` (header, string, example `12312312`)
- `X-Github-Event` (header, string, example `branch_protection_configuration`)
- `X-Github-Hook-Installation-Target-Id` (header, string, example `123123`)
- `X-Github-Hook-Installation-Target-Type` (header, string, example `repository`)
- `X-GitHub-Delivery` (header, string, example UUID-like `0b989ba4-242f-11e5-81e1-c7b6966d2516`)
- `X-Hub-Signature-256` (header, string, example `sha256=6dcb09b5b57875f334f61aebed695e2e4193db5e`)

Request body:
- Required: `true`
- Media type: `application/json`
- Top-level schema:
  - `title`: `branch protection configuration disabled event`
  - `type: object`
  - Properties:

1) `action`
- Type: `string`
- Enum: `["disabled"]` (indicates branch protection configuration was disabled)

2) `enterprise` (when webhook is configured on an enterprise account or an org within an enterprise)
- Title: `Enterprise`
- Type: `object`
- Description: Enterprise account on GitHub.
- Required fields: `id`, `node_id`, `name`, `slug`, `html_url`, `created_at`, `updated_at`, `avatar_url`
- Properties include:
  - `description` (string, nullable) – short description
  - `html_url` (string, uri) – e.g. `https://github.com/enterprises/octo-business`
  - `website_url` (string, uri, nullable)
  - `id` (integer, example `42`)
  - `node_id` (string)
  - `name` (string, example `Octo Business`)
  - `slug` (string, URL slug, example `octo-business`)
  - `created_at`, `updated_at` (string, date-time, nullable, examples ISO timestamps)
  - `avatar_url` (string, uri)

3) `installation` (when event is sent to a GitHub App)
- Title: `Simple Installation`
- Type: `object`
- Description: GitHub App installation; present for app-configured webhooks.
- Required fields: `id`, `node_id`
- Properties:
  - `id` (integer, example `1`)
  - `node_id` (string, example `MDQ6VXNlcjU4MzIzMQ==`)

4) `organization`
- Title: `Organization Simple`
- Type: `object`
- Description: A GitHub organization, present when webhook is configured for an org or repo in an org.
- Required fields: `login`, `url`, `id`, `node_id`, `repos_url`, `events_url`, `hooks_url`, `issues_url`, `members_url`, `public_members_url`, `avatar_url`, `description`
- Properties:
  - `login` (string, example `github`)
  - `id` (integer, example `1`)
  - `node_id` (string)
  - `url` (string, uri, example `https://api.github.com/orgs/github`)
  - Various URLs: `repos_url`, `events_url`, `hooks_url`, `issues_url`, `members_url`, `public_members_url`
  - `avatar_url` (string, example GitHub image URL)
  - `description` (string, nullable, example `A great organization`)

5) `repository`
- Title: `Repository`
- Type: `object`
- Description: Repository in which the event occurred.
- Key properties:
  - Identification:
    - `id` (integer, int64, example `42`)
    - `node_id` (string)
    - `name` (string, repo name, example `Team Environment`)
    - `full_name` (string, `owner/name`, example `octocat/Hello-World`)
  - `license` (License Simple, nullable):
    - `key` (string, e.g. `mit`)
    - `name` (string, e.g. `MIT License`)
    - `url` (string, uri, nullable)
    - `spdx_id` (string, nullable, e.g. `MIT`)
    - `node_id` (string)
    - `html_url` (string, uri)
  - `organization` (Simple User, nullable; org owner):
    - `name`, `email` (string, nullable)
    - `login` (string, e.g. `octocat`)
    - `id` (integer, int64)
    - `node_id` (string)
    - `avatar_url` (string, uri)
    - `gravatar_id` (string, nullable)
    - Multiple profile URLs: `url`, `html_url`, `followers_url`, `following_url`, `gists_url`, `starred_url`, `subscriptions_url`, `organizations_url`, `repos_url`, `events_url`, `received_events_url`
    - `type` (string, e.g. `User`)
    - `site_admin` (boolean)
    - `starred_at` (string timestamp)
    - `user_view_type` (string, e.g. `public`)
  - `forks` (integer)
  - `permissions`:
    - `admin`, `pull`, `triage`, `push`, `maintain` (boolean; `admin`, `pull`, `push` required)
  - `owner` (Simple User, required; same structure as above)
  - `private` (boolean, default `false`; repo visibility)
  - `html_url` (string, uri, example `https://github.com/octocat/Hello-World`)
  - `description` (string, nullable, e.g. `This your first repo!`)
  - `fork` (boolean; whether repo is a fork)
  - `url` (string, uri, API base)
  - Standard REST URLs/endpoints: `archive_url`, `assignees_url`, `blobs_url`, `branches_url`, `collaborators_url`, `comments_url`, `commits_url`, `compare_url`, `contents_url`, `contributors_url`, `deployments_url`, `downloads_url`, `events_url`, `forks_url`, `git_commits_url`, `git_refs_url`, `git_tags_url`, `git_url`, `issue_comment_url`, `issue_events_url`, `issues_url`, `keys_url`, `labels_url`, `languages_url`, `merges_url`, `milestones_url`, `notifications_url`, `pulls_url`, `releases_url`, `ssh_url`, `stargazers_url`, `statuses_url`, `subscribers_url`, `subscription_url`, `tags_url`, `teams_url`, `trees_url`, `clone_url`, `mirror_url` (nullable), `hooks_url`, `svn_url`, `homepage` (uri).

This excerpt primarily defines metadata for the GitHub v3 REST API and a single webhook event (`branch_protection_configuration` with action `disabled`) including required headers and the JSON payload structure (enterprise, installation, organization, and repository objects).

       - `url`: string (uri), nullable
       - `spdx_id`: string, nullable
       - `node_id`: string
       - `html_url`: string (uri)
       - `required`: `key`, `name`, `url`, `spdx_id`, `node_id`
     - `organization` (ref_19, nullable) – Simple User object describing owning org/user:
       - `name`, `email`: string, nullable
       - `login`: string
       - `id`: integer (int64)
       - `node_id`: string
       - `avatar_url`, `url`, `html_url`, `followers_url`, `subscriptions_url`, `organizations_url`, `repos_url`, `received_events_url`: string (uri)
       - `gravatar_id`: string, nullable
       - `following_url`, `gists_url`, `starred_url`, `events_url`: string with template segments
       - `type`: string, e.g. `User`
       - `site_admin`: boolean

       - `required`: avatar/profile/URL fields, `id`, `node_id`, `login`, `site_admin`, `type`
     - `forks`: integer
     - `permissions`:
       - `admin`, `pull`, `triage`, `push`, `maintain`: boolean
       - `required`: `admin`, `pull`, `push`
     - `owner` (ref_0) – same Simple User schema as ref_19, required.
     - `private`: boolean; whether repo is private, default `false`
     - `html_url`: string (uri)
     - `description`: string, nullable
     - `fork`: boolean
     - `url`: string (uri)
     - Standard GitHub REST sub-URLs (all strings, many with URI format and/or path templates):
       - `archive_url`, `assignees_url`, `blobs_url`, `branches_url`,
         `collaborators_url`, `comments_url`, `commits_url`, `compare_url`,
         `contents_url`, `contributors_url`, `deployments_url`, `downloads_url`,
         `events_url`, `forks_url`, `git_commits_url`, `git_refs_url`,

         `milestones_url`, `notifications_url`, `pulls_url`, `releases_url`,
         `ssh_url`, `stargazers_url`, `statuses_url`, `subscribers_url`,

llm
nodes.output:
OpenAI’s REST API v2.3.0 (`https://api.openai.com`) exposes 241 endpoints grouped by feature area. Full per-endpoint docs: `https://oapis.org/summary/openai/[idOrRoute]` and `https://platform.openai.com/docs/api-reference`.

ASSISTANTS  
Build assistants that orchestrate models, tools, and conversations via threads, messages, and runs.  
- Assistants: create, list, retrieve, modify, and delete assistants (`listAssistants`, `createAssistant`, `getAssistant`, `modifyAssistant`, `deleteAssistant`).  
- Threads: create, retrieve, modify, and delete conversation threads (`createThread`, `createThreadAndRun`, `getThread`, `modifyThread`, `deleteThread`).  
- Messages: list, create, retrieve, modify, delete messages in a thread (`listMessages`, `createMessage`, `getMessage`, `modifyMessage`, `deleteMessage`).  
- Runs: list, create, retrieve, modify, cancel runs (`listRuns`, `createRun`, `getRun`, `modifyRun`, `cancelRun`), plus list/retrieve run steps (`listRunSteps`, `getRunStep`).  
- Tool outputs: submit tool call results when a run requires them (`submitToolOuputsToRun`).

AUDIO  
Convert between text and audio, and manage voice consents/voices.  
- Text-to-speech: `createSpeech` generates audio or streams audio events from input text.  
- Speech-to-text: `createTranscription` transcribes audio in the source language, returning JSON/diarized/verbose JSON or streaming events; `createTranslation` translates audio to English.  
- Voice consent recordings: list, create, retrieve, update metadata, delete (`listVoiceConsents`, `createVoiceConsent`, `getVoiceConsent`, `updateVoiceConsent`, `deleteVoiceConsent`).  
- Custom voices: `createVoice` creates a custom voice.

AUDIT LOGS  
Organization-level auditing.  
- `list-audit-logs`: list user actions and configuration changes within the organization.

BATCH  
Run large numbers of requests asynchronously.  
- `listBatches`, `createBatch` (from uploaded request file), `retrieveBatch`, `cancelBatch` (moves to `cancelling` then `cancelled`, partial output available).

CERTIFICATES  
Manage TLS or similar certificates at org/project levels (limits: 50 per org; activate/deactivate up to 10 atomically).  
- Org-level: list, upload (does not auto-activate), activate, deactivate, get, modify name, delete inactive (`listOrganizationCertificates`, `uploadCertificate`, `activateOrganizationCertificates`, `deactivateOrganizationCertificates`, `getCertificate`, `modifyCertificate`, `deleteCertificate`).  
- Project-level: list, activate, deactivate (`listProjectCertificates`, `activateProjectCertificates`, `deactivateProjectCertificates`).

CHAT  
Legacy-style chat completions and their stored objects.  
- Storage: list stored chat completions, get one, update metadata only, delete (`listChatCompletions`, `getChatCompletion`, `updateChatCompletion`, `deleteChatCompletion`, `getChatCompletionMessages`). Only items created with `store: true` appear.  
- Generation: `createChatCompletion` generates chat responses (text, vision, audio). Behavior and parameter support vary by model, especially reasoning models; streaming supported. OpenAI recommends the newer Responses API for new projects.

COMPLETIONS  
Prompt-based completion (pre-chat style).  
- `createCompletion`: generate one or more completions; can stream and optionally return token probabilities.

CONVERSATIONS  
Higher-level conversation objects separate from threads/messages.  
- Items: list, create, get, delete items in a conversation (`listConversationItems`, `createConversationItems`, `getConversationItem`, `deleteConversationItem`).  
- Conversations: create, get, update, delete (deletion does not remove items) (`createConversation`, `getConversation`, `updateConversation`, `deleteConversation`).

EMBEDDINGS  
Vector representations for ML/retrieval.  
- `createEmbedding`: generate embedding vectors for input text.

EVALS  
Define, manage, and run automated evaluations of models.  
- Evaluations: list, create (structure/config, grading types, data sources), get, update, delete (`listEvals`, `createEval`, `getEval`, `updateEval`, `deleteEval`).  
- Runs: list runs for an eval, create run with chosen datasource/model config, get, cancel, delete (`getEvalRuns`, `createEvalRun`, `getEvalRun`, `cancelEvalRun`, `deleteEvalRun`).  
- Outputs: list and get specific run output items (`getEvalRunOutputItems`, `getEvalRunOutputItem`).  
- See Evals guide for details.

FILES  
Central file storage used by Assistants, Fine-tuning, Batch, etc.  
- Capabilities: list, upload, retrieve metadata, delete (removes from vector stores), download (`listFiles`, `createFile`, `retrieveFile`, `deleteFile`, `downloadFile`).  
- Limits: file size up to 512 MB, 2.5 TB total per project; no org-wide limit.  
- Special constraints:  
  - Assistants: up to 2M tokens and specific file types.  
  - Fine-tuning: `.jsonl` only, specific formats per model type.  
  - Batch: `.jsonl` up to 200 MB with specific request format.  
- Contact OpenAI to increase storage limits.

FINE-TUNING  
Manage fine-tuning jobs, checkpoints, and permissions, plus graders.  
- Graders: `runGrader` (execute a grader) and `validateGrader`.  
- Checkpoint permissions (admin API key only): list, create (share models across projects), delete (`listFineTuningCheckpointPermissions`, `createFineTuningCheckpointPermission`, `deleteFineTuningCheckpointPermission`).  
- Jobs: list all jobs, create job, retrieve, cancel, list checkpoints, list events, pause/resume (`listPaginatedFineTuningJobs`, `createFineTuningJob`, `retrieveFineTuningJob`, `cancelFineTuningJob`, `listFineTuningJobCheckpoints`, `listFineTuningEvents`, `pauseFineTuningJob`, `resumeFineTuningJob`). Responses include job status and final fine-tuned model name.

GROUPS, USERS, ROLES (ORG LEVEL)  
Manage org groups, group membership, and role assignments.  
- Groups: list, create, update, delete (`list-groups`, `create-group`, `update-group`, `delete-group`).  
- Group users: list users in a group, add user, remove user (`list-group-users`, `add-group-user`, `remove-group-user`).  
- Group org-role assignments: list, assign, unassign roles for groups (`list-group-role-assignments`, `assign-group-role`, `unassign-group-role`).  
- Roles (org): list roles, create custom role, update, delete (`list-roles`, `create-role`, `update-role`, `delete-role`).

IMAGES  
Image generation and editing.  
- Create: `createImage` generates images from prompts (GPT Image and DALL·E models).  
- Edit/extend: `createImageEdit` edits or extends input images with a prompt (supports GPT Image models and `dall-e-2`).  
- Variations: `createImageVariation` creates variants of an input image (DALL·E 2 only).

INVITES  
Manage organization membership invitations.  
- `list-invites`, `inviteUser` (create invite requiring recipient acceptance), `retrieve-invite`, `delete-invite` (not if already accepted).

MODELS  
Model catalog management.  
- `listModels`: list all available models with owner and availability.  
- `retrieveModel`: get model metadata and permissioning.  
- `deleteModel`: delete a fine-tuned model (Owner role required).

MODERATIONS  
Safety classification for text and images.  
- `createModeration`: classify content as potentially harmful; see moderation guide.

PROJECTS, PROJECT ACCESS, AND PROJECT ROLES  
Project-level organization of keys, rate limits, users, groups, and roles.  

Projects:  
- `list-projects`, `create-project` (cannot delete, only archive), `retrieve-project`, `modify-project`, `archive-project`.  

Project API keys and service accounts:  
- Keys: list, retrieve, delete (`list-project-api-keys`, `retrieve-project-api-key`, `delete-project-api-key`), with confirmation or error if key is for service account.  
- Service accounts: list, create (returns unredacted API key), retrieve, delete (`list-project-service-accounts`, `create-project-service-account`, `retrieve-project-service-account`, `delete-project-service-account`); deletion may fail if project is archived.  

Project users:  
- List, add (must already be org members), retrieve, modify role, delete (`list-project-users`, `create-project-user`, `retrieve-project-user`, `modify-project-user`, `delete-project-user`); deletion fails if project is archived.  

Project group access:  
- Project groups: list groups with access, add group, remove group (`list-project-groups`, `add-project-group`, `remove-project-group`).  
- Project group role assignments: list, assign, unassign roles for groups in a project (`list-project-group-role-assignments`, `assign-project-group-role`, `unassign-project-group-role`).  

Project user role assignments:  
- `list-project-user-role-assignments`, `assign-project-user-role`, `unassign-project-user-role`.  

Project roles:  
- List project roles, create custom role, update, delete (`list-project-roles`, `create-project-role`, `update-project-role`, `delete-project-role`).

REALTIME  
Realtime API over WebRTC/SIP, plus ephemeral client credentials.  
- Calls (WebRTC/SIP): create call over WebRTC (get SDP answer), accept incoming SIP, hang up, transfer via SIP REFER, reject with SIP status (`create-realtime-call`, `accept-realtime-call`, `hangup-realtime-call`, `refer-realtime-call`, `reject-realtime-call`).  
- Client auth tokens:  
  - `create-realtime-client-secret`: create short-lived client secret plus effective session config, for WebRTC auth without exposing main API key.  
  - `create-realtime-session`: create ephemeral API token and session object for realtime usage; includes `client_secret`.  
  - `create-realtime-transcription-session`: same idea but specialized for realtime transcription sessions.  

RESPONSES  
Recommended unified interface for text, image, tools, and background responses.  
- `createResponse`: create a model response from text/image inputs, with optional tool calls (custom code, web search, file search) and structured output (JSON).  
- `getResponse`: retrieve by ID.  
- `deleteResponse`: delete by ID.  
- `cancelResponse`: cancel a background response (`background: true` only); see background guide.  
- `listInputItems`: list input items associated with a response.

SKILLS  
Manage reusable “skills” and their versions for a project.  
- Skills: list, create, get by ID, update default version, delete (`ListSkills`, `CreateSkill`, `GetSkill`, `UpdateSkillDefaultVersion`, `DeleteSkill`).  
- Skill contents: download skill zip bundle (`GetSkillContent`).  
- Skill versions: list versions, create new immutable version, get a version (`ListSkillVersions`, `CreateSkillVersion`, `GetSkillVersion`).

nodes.output:
The provided content is an overview of the GitHub v3 REST API (version 1.1.4) focused on the `actions` category of endpoints. These endpoints allow full management of GitHub Actions at enterprise, organization, and repository levels, covering configuration, security, runners, caches, artifacts, and workflow runs. The API base URL is `https://api.github.com`, and more detailed per‑endpoint docs are available at `https://oapis.org/summary/github/[idOrRoute]`.

Key capability areas within `actions`:

- Enterprise‑level cache configuration:
  - Get and set GitHub Actions cache retention and storage limits for an enterprise.

- Organization‑level cache, runners, and permissions:
  - Get and set cache retention and storage limits for an organization.
  - View total Actions cache usage and usage by repository within an organization.
  - Manage GitHub‑hosted runners for an organization:
    - List and create GitHub‑hosted runners.
    - List, get, and delete custom images and image versions for hosted runners.
    - Retrieve GitHub‑owned and partner images, machine specs, and supported platforms.
    - Get, update, and delete specific hosted runners.
    - Get limits for GitHub‑hosted runners.
  - Configure organization‑wide GitHub Actions permissions:
    - Get and set general Actions permissions.
    - Get and set artifact/log retention settings.
    - Get and set fork PR contributor approval policy.
    - Get and set workflow settings for private repo forks.
    - Manage which repositories are enabled for Actions (list, set, enable, disable).
    - Get and set allowed actions and reusable workflows.
    - Get and set self‑hosted runner settings and which repositories can use them.
    - Get and set default workflow permissions for the organization.

- Organization self‑hosted runner groups and access:
  - List, create, get, update, and delete self‑hosted runner groups.
  - Manage group membership:
    - List GitHub‑hosted runners in a group.
    - List, set, add, and remove repository access to a runner group.
    - List, set, add, and remove self‑hosted runners belonging to a group.
  - Manage organization self‑hosted runners:
    - List runners and runner applications.
    - Generate just‑in‑time runner config.
    - Create registration and removal tokens.
    - Get and delete specific self‑hosted runners.
    - List and manage labels for self‑hosted runners (list, add, set, remove all, remove one).

- Organization secrets and variables:
  - Secrets:
    - List organization secrets.
    - Retrieve the organization public key used to encrypt secrets.
    - Get, create/update, and delete individual organization secrets.
    - Manage which repositories can access a secret (list, set, add, remove).
  - Variables:
    - List, create, get, update, and delete organization‑level variables.
    - Manage which repositories can access a variable (list, set, add, remove).

- Repository artifacts and caches:
  - Artifacts:
    - List artifacts for a repository.
    - Get, delete, and download individual artifacts.
  - Caches:
    - Get and set cache retention and storage limits for a repository.
    - Get cache usage for a repository.
    - List caches for a repository.
    - Delete caches by cache key or by cache ID.

- Job and workflow run management:
  - Individual jobs:
    - Get a job for a workflow run.
    - Download job logs.
    - Re‑run a specific job.
  - OIDC customization:
    - Get and set the OIDC subject claim customization template for a repository.
  - Repository‑level org resources:
    - List organization secrets accessible from a repository.
    - List organization variables accessible from a repository.
  - Repository Actions permissions and security:
    - Get and set Actions permissions for a repository.
    - Get and set access level for workflows external to the repository.
    - Get and set artifact/log retention at repository level.
    - Get and set fork PR contributor approval requirements.
    - Get and set private repo fork workflow settings.
    - Get and set which actions and reusable workflows are allowed.
    - Get and set default workflow permissions for the repository.
  - Repository self‑hosted runners:
    - List self‑hosted runners and runner applications for a repository.
    - Generate JIT configuration for a runner.
    - Create registration and removal tokens.
    - Get and delete specific self‑hosted runners.
    - Manage runner labels (list, add, set, remove all, remove one).

- Workflow runs and logs:
  - List workflow runs for a repository.
  - Get and delete specific workflow runs.
  - View and manage review history for workflow runs (e.g., approvals for fork PRs).
  - List artifacts for a workflow run.
  - Get a particular workflow run attempt.
  - List jobs for a specific attempt.
  - Download logs for a run attempt.
  - Cancel or force‑cancel a workflow run.
  - Use custom deployment protection rules (custom gates) and review them.
  - List jobs for a run and download or delete workflow run logs.
  - Get pending deployments associated with a run and review/approve or reject them.
  - Re‑run workflows (entire workflow or only failed jobs).
  - Get usage metrics for a workflow run.

- Repository secrets and variables:
  - Secrets:
    - List repository secrets.
    - Get the repository public key.
    - Get, create/update, and delete repository secrets.
  - Variables:
    - List repository variables.
    - Create repository variables. (Text cuts off here but implies similar get/update/delete operations.)

Overall, this `actions` section exposes fine‑grained REST endpoints to:

- Configure GitHub Actions behavior and permissions at enterprise, organization, and repository scopes.
- Manage GitHub‑hosted and self‑hosted runners, including groups, images, labels, and tokens.
- Control caches, artifacts, logs, and retention policies.
- Work with secrets, variables, and OIDC settings for secure workflows.
- Inspect, control, and re‑run workflows and jobs, including approvals, deployment gates, and usage statistics.
